briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. I will now begin the climate briefing for April 16, 2026.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Change Response Week and Expanded Government Investment
sections.content: Domestically today, various local governments and organizations, including Gangdong-gu, Dangjin-si, Pocheon-si, Gwangju Urban Corporation, and Changwon Special City, are operating 'Climate Change Week' in observance of Earth Day, raising awareness of climate action [1, 2, 4, 6, 7, 11, 13, 14, 16, 17, 34]. During this period, Namhae-gun, Seongbuk-gu, Samcheok-si, and others are encouraging active citizen participation through resident-involved carbon neutrality practice campaigns, '10-minute lights-out' events, and experience and education programs [8, 9, 10, 12, 15, 18]. Furthermore, the government has expressed its national commitment by deciding to invest 3.4 trillion won, a 14.1% increase from the previous year, to address climate change [5], and announced the existence of the Presidential Committee on National Climate Crisis Response [19]. Internationally, the Mayor of Los Angeles has announced a new plan to combat climate change [60], and the U.S. Department of Agriculture has restored climate change-related subsidies [63], indicating an expansion of interest and investment in climate action both domestically and abroad.

sections.title: 🌱 Strengthening Public-Private Cooperation for Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Public-private cooperation is actively underway to achieve carbon neutrality goals. KTR is supporting the realization of carbon neutrality in industrial complexes [20, 40], and in the Chungju health and medical sector, an MOU has been signed and training conducted for fostering talent in carbon neutrality and ESG management [22]. Notably, Hyundai Department Store Group, in cooperation with the Northern Regional Forest Service, is accelerating climate action by promoting the creation of a 'Carbon Neutral Forest' project, planting 40,000 trees in Yongin-si [23, 24, 31, 32, 37, 38]. At the local government level, Hwaseong Special City's Carbon Neutrality Support Center is holding regular meetings [25], and Gwangmyeong-si is encouraging citizen participation by awarding up to 7 million won to excellent apartments practicing carbon neutrality [30]. In the education sector, Gunsan Office of Education is creating an eco-friendly commuting environment by expanding electric school vehicles [26], and Jinsang Elementary School is leading ecological transition education by operating as a carbon-neutral pilot school [29], highlighting comprehensive efforts.

sections.title: ⚠️ Challenges and Domestic/International Paradoxes of Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy, despite significant progress, faces several challenges and paradoxes. Domestically, power generation subsidiary unions, while agreeing with the energy transition, oppose the establishment of a renewable energy generation corporation and discussions on the merger and acquisition of public power generation companies, demanding continuous communication for a 'just transition' [41, 42, 46, 49]. Amidst this, various complementary renewable energy technologies such as solar, SMR (Small Modular Reactors), hydrogen, and wind power are being explored [43, 47, 50, 57], and regional discussions are active, with the Yeongsan River Basin Environmental Office hosting a forum on the major renewable energy transition in the Gwangju-Jeonnam region [44, 59]. However, internationally, warnings have been raised about a 'paradoxical' situation where China benefits from the renewable energy transition process [45, 52], and Europe's tightening eco-friendly construction regulations demand new preparations from Korean companies [58]. Furthermore, the news that the UK plans to abolish carbon taxes on electricity generation demonstrates the complexity of energy policy [85], suggesting that the renewable energy transition is not merely a technical issue but an economic, social, and geopolitical challenge.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Impacts of Climate Change and International Warnings
sections.content: The impacts of climate change are becoming more severe, and warnings about them are growing louder worldwide. UCLA researchers have published findings indicating that climate change is contributing to an increase in homelessness across the United States, particularly in areas including LA, emphasizing its link to social vulnerability [3, 68]. Scientists have discovered hidden oceanic methane sources that could exacerbate global warming, raising additional concerns [62], and tragic news has emerged from Colombia that glaciers have disappeared due to global warming [72]. Furthermore, climate change is intensifying seasonal allergy symptoms [61], increasing encounters between humans and wildlife [65, 70], and threatening Norway's aquaculture industry [71], exerting widespread impacts across ecosystems and human life. Policy reversals and legal uncertainties, such as the Trump administration's Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) overturning a landmark climate change ruling [69] and California's climate change lawsuit being halted during Supreme Court review [73], are also hindering climate change response.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has clearly demonstrated the diverse domestic and international efforts to address climate change, alongside the escalating threats of climate change and the complex challenges they entail. At this juncture, international cooperation and swift action are more critical than ever for a sustainable future. This concludes today's climate briefing.
